div.p: 1/Z p is constrained, we will use this equation instead to find the appropriate reference impedance for the network,
div.formula: Z ref = 1 Y ref = g 1 ω 0 ∆ω Z p = 31.8 Ω. (77
div.p: Using Z ref above in Eq. ( 46), we can now find the impedance of the series "auxiliary" resonator:
div.formula: Z ser = g 2 ω 0 ∆ω Z ref = 74.4 Ω.(78)
div.p: Finally the load impedance, Eq. ( 47), is denormalized to
div.formula: Z L = Z ref /g 3 = 28.
div.p: 8 Ω. To transform Z L to the Z 0 = 50 Ω environment, we insert an admittance inverter, J in Fig. 21(c), whose value is

For completeness, before proceeding to the implementation of the circuit shown in Fig. 21(d), we will show how the same circuit parameters can be derived using an approach based on the method in Sec. IV B. We start from the same prototype coefficients and the constrained value of Z p above, and construct the circuit of Fig. 21(a), where the inverter values can be calculated in the usual way Eqs. (49)(50)(51) and assuming some arbitrary value for Z 2 . Next, we factor J 23 into a series combination of J 12 and J = 1 Z0 J12 J23 , as shown in Fig. 21(b), and transform the shunt resonator Z 2 and the adjacent inverters into a series resonator, Z ser = 1/J 2 12 Z 2 = g 1 g 2 Z p /w 2 , to arrive again at Fig. 21(c). The values of Z ser and J found here are identical to those found above using the direct ladder synthesis.

Proceeding with the circuit implementation of Ref. 6, the series resonator was implemented as a half-wave transmission line resonator with impedance Z λ/2 , and the inverter J is implemented as a quarter wave transmission line with impedance Z λ/4 . The two transmission lines are in series with each other as shown in Fig. 21(d).
The impedance Z seen looking through the series resonator Z ser towards a load Z L = 1/J 2 Z 0 (Fig. 21(c)) near the resonance frequency, can be approximated as (writing ω = ω 0 + δω):
div.formula: Z = Z L + 2jZ ser δω ω 0 .(80)
div.p: Likewise, the impedance Z seen through a half wave transmission line with Z λ/2 in series with a quarter-wave transmission line with Z λ/4 (Fig. 21(d)) near the resonance frequency ω 0 is
this is the basis for Eq. (S40) in Ref. 6 and Eq. (2) in Ref. 66. Equating Eq. ( 80) and (81), we get the following quadratic equation for Z λ/2 which we can solve to get the impedance value:
where Z λ/4 = 1/J (Eq. 79), and Z ser found in Eq. (78).
Using the numerical values in Eq. ( 78) and (79), we finally get Z λ/2 = 54.55 Ω and Z λ/4 = 37.93 Ω. This agrees with the values of Z λ/2 ≈ 58 Ω and Z λ/4 ≈ 40 Ω, reported in Ref. 6.

Figure 22 shows harmonic balance simulations using a symbolic nonlinear device model in Keysight ADS for the pumped SQUID. All simulation curves use the same flux bias Φ dc = 0.3075 Φ 0 (chosen to get the shunted FIG. 22. ADS harmonic balance simulations of the circuits in Fig. 21(a-c), and the transmission line implementations, Fig. 21(d), with the parameters indicated in the legend.

SQUID resonance frequency to 6 GHz), and pump amplitude Φ ac = 0.111 Φ 0 . The curve labeled (a-c) (red) corresponds to the schematics in Fig. 21(a)-(c), where the inverters are implemented as ideal Y-matrices. These circuits produce identical results showing that the transformations in Fig. 21(a)-(c) are equivalent. The blue trace (d) represents a half-wave/quarter-wave implementation as in Ref. 6 corresponding to the schematic in Fig. 21(d), with the transmission line impedances as obtained in this section. The two traces should ideally be equivalent, however, it is not surprising that the trace (d) shows slightly narrower response than the transformations in (a-c), since it is using approximations that become worse with detuning away from ω 0 . The synthesized parameters and simulated response agree with what was reported in Ref. 6, showing that their matched broadband parametric amplifier design can be understood in terms of simple network synthesis methods known from microwave engineering.

div.head: Calculating the parametric amplifier pump amplitude
div.p: In Section III C we saw that sum-frequency parametric coupling (amplification process) presents the input port with an effective negative resistance, resulting in gain. This negative resistance, as we have seen in Eqs. (39)(40)(41) and Fig. 8, depends on the pump amplitude. Earlier in this Section, we designed matching networks for parametric amplifiers, assuming that the pump amplitude (or alternatively, the parametric coupling strength β p ) has been set correctly so that the negative impedance -|R P A | presented to the matching network satisfies the so-called decrement condition, Eq. ( 53):
where g 0 and g 1 are the prototype coefficients, w is the fractional bandwidth, and Z 1 the impedance of the resonance embedding the modulated inductor.

In the coupled-mode EoM matrix picture, the pump amplitude is expressed by the dimensionless parametric coupling strength β p . We can calculate it as the normalized dissipation rate of resonator Z 1 due to the effective resistance |R P A |:
div.formula: β p = ω 0 2γ 0 Z 1 |R P A | = 1 2 g N g N +1 g 0 g 1 ,(84)
div.p: where we have used γ 0 = ∆ω/g N g N +1 , Eq. ( 62), and the decrement relation, Eq. ( 83). As a simple check, for the 2-mode parametric amplifier of Fig. 2(c) with 20 dB gain, N = 1 and, using g 0 = 1.0 and g 2 = 0.9045 calculated according to Appendix E, we get from Eq. (84) that β p = g2 2g0 = 0.452, which recovers the result of Eq. (22).

In a circuit implementation, the pump amplitude is expressed via the parameter α, defined in Figs. 8(b) and 9(b). Using the shunt representation in these figures, we can calculate α for a given prototype, signal power gain G, and fractional bandwidth w:
div.formula: α = √ G -1 √ G + 1 w g 1 2 × g 2 N +1 ; N ∈ even g -2 N +1 ; N ∈ odd ,(85)
div.p: as shown in Appendix C. Eqs. 84 and 85 are useful in circuit design and simulation. Eq. 85 in particular should be used to ensure that the chosen physical implementation of the parametric coupler can deliver the required modulation strength. Knowledge of this quantity can also inform the design of the experimental setup, with respect to e.g., choice of pump generator, attenuation in the microwave cable delivering the pump, or on-chip coupling architecture. It can also serve as a starting point for experimentally finding the optimal pump power during the amplifier bringup and calibration procedure.

div.head: E. Design flow
div.p: As a summary, we outline a general design flow that we have found useful in prototyping and simulating parametrically coupled networks.

div.head: Draw the network graph.
div.p: For a given required functionality, we find it useful to first sketch the coupling graph, Sec. II B. Doing so we can readily identify the required coupling processes, plan the mode basis, and assign mode frequencies and ports.

3. Assign coupling rates. Given a bandwidth requirement for the circuit, calculate the port dissipation rates γ k using the prototype coefficients and Eqs. ( 61) and (62). Calculate the normalization rate γ 0 from Eq. ( 10), and the coupling terms β jk from Eq. ( 63). For networks involving parametric amplification, use Eq. (84) to calculate β P A that corresponds to the sum-frequency pump, as was done in Sec. V D.
4. Write down the EoM matrix. From the network graph, write down the EoM matrix using the rules in Fig. 1. This step, and indeed steps 2 and 3 as well, are amenable to automation.

Calculate S-parameters. The S-parameters of the design can now be calculated from the EoM matrix using Eq. ( 8) to verify the desired behavior of the circuit. It is easy at this step to explore, for example, the effects of different pump phases and amplitudes, or different network prototype coefficients.

div.head: Determine parametric coupler implementation.
div.p: The configuration of the parametric coupler will constrain the rest of the circuit. In a Josephson junction circuit, we have several options for the coupler, including a shunt-connected dc-SQUID (Sec. V B), an rf-SQUID coupler (Sec. V C), or a balanced bridge [39,67], each with their own advantages and ability to incorporate advanced nonlinear devices such as the SNAIL [8] or SQUID arrays [7]. With the implementation fixed, construct resonators to embed the coupler, e.g. by shunting the Josephson element with capacitors to achieve the specified resonance frequency. The impedances of the resonators embedding the coupler will be important parameters in the following steps. Use Eq. ( 85) or (C8) to ensure the required modulation strength is consistent with the coupler design.

7. Determine resonator implementation. With the resonators embedding the parametric coupler determined in step 6, next choose the implementation (e.g. lumped-element, quarter-wave, cavity) and impedances of the remaining resonators in the circuit based on the available technology.

8. Calculate immittance inverters. With all resonator impedances now known, use Eqs. ( 49)-( 51) to calculate the values of the admittance J inverters. If the resonators are not lumped, or the implementation calls for K inverters instead, refer to [57] for alternative expressions for inverter values. 9. Determine inverter implementation. For J inverters in a lumped-element circuit, choose either a capacitive or inductive circuit (Fig. 11) for each of the inverters, and calculate the component values. Capacitive implementations are favorable in Josephson circuits as they do not introduce additional superconducting loops. Inductive implementations can be advantageous in circuits that require transition between single-ended and balanced sections [67]. Other implementations are more appropriate for K inverters or for distributed components; these can be found in [28,48,57]. The parametric couplers can be approximated as a numerically modulated inductance or mutual inductance, varying at the pump frequency. This step is important as it will reveal any issues stemming from, e.g., the fact that the inverters are not ideal and their reactances are frequencydependent. Most tools allow for manual trimming of component values in real-time, and this functionality can be used to manually compensate for nonidealities in components. The designer should also explore here the effects of component variations and parasitics on the performance of the device.

At the completion of the above steps, the design should be ready for layout and manufacture. As noted, many of these steps can be automated based on the rules and equations given in this tutorial, allowing the designer to focus their creative effort on steps 1, 6, and the physical design.

div.head: VI. CONCLUSION AND OUTLOOK
div.p: The design of parametrically coupled networks and that of microwave filters both boil down to engineering the mutual coupling rates between resonant modes, and between these modes and the environment. These disciplines, however, approach this problem with seemingly different language, tools, and methods. In this tutorial we have shown that, in fact, a common language can be used to describe both electrical filter networks and parametric networks, regardless of their physical implementation.

We have shown that given prescribed response characteristics for a 1D-connected system of N modes, which can be fully described by N + 2 prototype coefficients, the coupling rates in the system can be calculated from these coefficients to realize the desired response. The correspondence we established between the β (coupling efficiency) and γ (dissipation rate) of the coupled-modes language, to the admittance (impedance) inverters J (K) of the filter synthesis language, offers agility in designing systems that may contain a mix of both passive and parametric couplings. Techniques developed for matching more complicated electrical systems, like ferrite circulators and amplifiers, as well as techniques for designing with internally lossy components, can now be readily used in the context of parametrically coupled devices. The principles outlined here also open the door for microwave engineers and filter designers to have significant impact on key technologies, components, and subsystems in the quantum computer stack.

We presented a range of example designs based on simple filter networks, however, more sophisticated multiplyconnected structures have been researched, both in the microwave filter arena [54,56] and in Josephson parametric amplifiers [68]. By bridging the gap between these disciplines we hope to spur further research that utilizes advanced filter synthesis methods to produce new and performant parametric amplifiers, transduction networks [61], and non-reciprocal device designs. Further research is also needed to expand on the methods outlined here to include systematic analysis and optimization of quantum noise performance of these networks.

The Z-matrix form is especially useful in helping to identify a convenient circuit representation. This becomes clear if we convert an arbitrary Z-matrix to a transmission (T -or ABCD) matrix [28]. One can show that the resulting transformation can be factored into three separate matrices,

From this form, we see that diagonal elements of the Zmatrix represent series impedances, while off-diagonal elements correspond to an inverter-like object sandwiched between these impedances, Fig. 23.

If we substitute into Eq. (A1) the Z-matrices we derived for the modulated inductor, Eq. ( 32) for parametric frequency conversion, and Eq. ( 38) for parametric amplification, we have the following transmission matrix equivalents,
div.formula: T ser L,FC = 1 jω 1 L 0 0 1 0 -jK * 1 -j/K 2 0 1 jω 2 L 0 0 1 ,(A2)
div.p: and
div.formula: T ser L,PA = 1 jω 1 L 0 0 1 0 -jK 1 j/K * 2 0 1 -jω 2 L 0 0 1 ,(A3)
div.p: where we have defined a complex inverter constant,
div.formula: K ≡ ω δL 2 . (A4
div.p: From both of these expressions, we see that the timeinvariant part of the inductance, L 0 , looks like a series inductor, with its impedance reckoned at both the signal (ω 1 ) and idler frequencies (±ω 2 ). Meanwhile, the inverter's role is to convert current at the signal/idler frequencies to voltage at the idler/signal frequencies. The strength of this conversion process is determined by the pump amplitude |δL|. In Figure 8(b), we denote this as the series representation for the parametrically modulated inductor.
The Josephson equations [69,70] yield a simple expression for the inductance of a single junction as a function of bias current I,

where Φ 0 ≈ 2.068 µA • nH is the single flux quantum, and I 0 is the junction critical current. This is a nonlinear function of the bias current and, if expanded around I = 0, in the small current limit this can be approximated as
div.formula: L J (I) L J0 1 + 12
div.p: where we define the
and inductance modulation amplitude
div.formula: δL ≡ L J02
div.p: a. 4-wave mixing. Eq. (B3) differs slightly from the expression in Eq. ( 28) having a modulation frequency 2ω P instead of ω P . In this configuration, where we're using a quadratic nonlinearity in the current, the inductance modulation is doubled in frequency. In other words, the nonlinear element is modulated by a subharmonic drive. Since, the purpose of this element is to achieve mixing between a signal and idler, we require only that the element modulate at the difference frequency between the two. Therefore, for frequency conversion we have 2ω P = |ω 1 -ω 2 | (connecting ω 1 and ω 2 ), and for amplification we have 2ω P = ω 1 + ω 2 (connecting ω 1 and -ω 2 ). This approach is often termed 4-wave mixing, but can be interpreted as a consequence of using a quadratic nonlinearity to achieve the required mixing product frequencies for parametric coupling.
b. 3-wave mixing. Alternatively, one can choose to add a dc-bias to the pump current, I P (t) = I dc + |I P | cos(ω P t + φ P ), in which case the lowest order expansion for the inductance is
div.formula: L 3w J (t) L J0 + δL cos (ω P t + φ P ),(B6)
div.p: with
div.formula: L J0 ≡ L J0 1 -I 2 dc /I 2 0 (B7) and δL ≡ L J0 I dc I 0 1 (1 -I 2 dc /I 2 0 ) 3/2 |I P |. (B8)
div.p: Eq. (B6) corresponds to 'direct' modulation of the inductance at ω P as in Eq. ( 28), a consequence of the dominant linear dependence of δL on current when expanded around a bias current.

While the current nonlinearity above is convenient to use, it requires some care in handling the large pump tone required to modulate the inductance, which can be close to the signal frequency in the case of 4-wave mixing, necessitating additional components in the signal path to cancel or filter it out. In practice, these extra components can impact the overall noise performance and complicate operation and bringup.

div.head: dc SQUID
div.p: As an alternative to using the current nonlinearity, one can utilize a loop topology that interferes two supercurrent paths. The result is an effective Josephson inductance that can be tuned by the magnetic flux threading the loop. There are several variants of these SQUID (superconducting quantum interference device) configurations, but in this work, for simplicity we utilize an ideal dc-SQUID. This is comprised of two Josephson junctions in parallel, with no stray inductance and a means to modulate its magnetic flux [70]. In the limit where stray inductance is neglible, the inductance has a simple form,
div.formula: L SQ (I, Φ) = L J0 (I) 2| cos (πΦ A /Φ 0 )| , (B9
div.p: where Φ A is the magnetic flux induced by an adjacent flux line, and can include both dc and ac components. L J0 (I) is the single junction current-dependent inductance, Eq. (B1). Eq. (B9) is a nonlinear, periodic function in the applied flux. As with the Josephson current nonlinearity, one can likewise choose to bias such that the dominant first order expansion term in flux is either quadratic/4-wave (Φ dc = 0) or linear/3-wave (|Φ dc | = 0 modulo (n + 1/2)Φ 0 for integer n). Just as we did for the current-pumped junction, we can likewise expand the inductance for a given flux pump modulation, Φ A (t) = Φ dc +Φ P cos (ω P t + φ P ). One can then generate both 4-wave (Eq. (B3)) and 3wave (Eq. (B6)) inductance modulation forms as we did above [49].
Appendix C: Using the J/K-inverter models of parametric coupling in amplifier design

In Figs. 8 and9, we presented equivalent models for the parametrically-modulated inductor and mutual as impedance (K) or admittance (J) inverters. Here we will show how to use these 'parametric inverter' models as aids in the synthesis of wideband amplifier circuits, as well as in the systematic calculation of the full 2-port response response of these circuits. In addition, we will show how these inverter models can be used to set the parametric modulation strength, or pump amplitude, based on the bandwidth and response characteristics in a multi-pole matched amplifier.

As noted in Sec. III C, the immitance inverter models reduce to the 'pumpistor model' [49] for reflection JPAs; however, they are more general in that they allow one to obtain full 2-port scattering parameters, since they treat the signal and idler ports equally. To be more concrete, in the degenerate parametric amplifier case, the incoming signal and idler tones are driven from the same physical port and then both re-emitted from the same physical port (see Fig. 24(a, left)). This can still be described as a 2-port scattering problem in the signal/idler basis, where the signal is the 'left' frequency port and the idler is the 'right' frequency port, as illustrated in Fig. 24(a,middle). Likewise, any matching network (e.g., Refs. [6,7]) would be mirrored on either side, but evaluated at the respective frequencies: ω s on the signal side and -ω i (= ω s -ω P ) on the idler side. In a nondegenerate amplifier, the distinction between physical and frequency ports is not necessary, and the signal/idler matching networks can be physically distinct with different numbers of poles and filter types, allowing for more flexibility in design.

div.head: Degenerate parametric amplifier
div.p: In Figs. 8 and9, we labeled the inverter equivalents as K-type or J-type, depending on the topology of the circuit in which they are embedded. When designing a degenerate parametric amplifier around a flux-or current-pumped Josephson circuit, it is natural to resonate the Josephson circuit out using a parallel capacitor, Fig. 24(a,left). In this case, since the parametrically modulated component is embedded in a parallel resonator, it is more convenient to use the 'shunt representation' in Figs. 8 and9, and model the parametric coupler as an admittance J-inverter, Fig. 24(a,right). The full circuit can be analyzed by virtually mirroring it and evaluating each element at the signal and idler frequencies (Fig. 24(b-c)). Note that we have included the possibility of stray inductance L 1e in the transformation to a 2-frequency-port circuit. We can then write out the total transmission (ABCD) matrix for this example by cascading the ABCD matrices of the constituent components. For the single-pole amplifier in Fig. 24(a) we have, between the parametrically modulated inductor tee and modulated mutual inductance circuits, and their K-and J-inverters counterparts. The inverter formulae are given in Fig. 9. (c) Example multipole matching strategy for a nondegenerate parametric amplifier using a parametric J-inverter. The tee-equivalent J inverter transforms the SQUID-embedded doubleresonator circuit into effective parallel LC sections of multipole signal/idler matching networks (m.n. in the figure). The input admittance looking into the inverter from the signal circuit, Ys,in(ωs) is the nondegenerate equivalent to the pumpistor model admittance [49] since Y * i (ωi) = Yi(-ωi).
div.formula: T Σ = 1 0 jω s C 1 1 1 0 (jω s L 1 ) -1 1 0 j/J s * -jJ i 0 1 0 (-jω i L 1 ) -1 1 1 0 -jω i C 1 1 ,(C1)
div.p: where the inverter constants J s/i are defined in Fig. 8. From Eq. (C1), and assuming a port impedance Z 2 as in Fig. 24(a), one can compute the scattering matrix [28], whose elements express the reflection (cis-)gains and conversion (trans-)gains vs. frequency.

div.head: Nondegenerate amplifier
div.p: The single-pole design in the above section is simple, and can be easily extended to a nondegenerate amplifier. One starts with a modulated inductance with a value that is constrained by its physical realization (e.g., available critical current densities in a given process), and the center frequency of the amplifier can then be set by the value of C 1 . The value of the terminating impedance Z 2 can be chosen to yield the desired bandwidth, ∆ω = 1/2πZ 2 C 1 . In turn, Z 2 can be realized by transforming the 50 Ω source impedance using an immitance inverter (see Appendix D). In designs using multi-pole matching networks, the relative immittances of each of the sections can be systematically determined by the filter prototypes. In Section V D 1 we designed a coupled-mode network for a matched nondegenerate amplifier, but abstracted the physical details of the parametric coupler. Here we demonstrate a physical circuit synthesis procedure using the method of Sec. IV A and the parametric inverter model from Sec. III. Furthermore, whereas in Sec. V D 1 we assumed the same prototype and bandwidth was used for both signal and idler ports, here we treat a more general synthesis problem that does not make this assumption.

We start with the circuit shown in Fig. 24(b), left, as the core of our design: an inductor tee where the center inductance is the modulated inductor (e.g., a Josephson circuit) and the arm inductances are required to form distinct tank circuits that make up the first resonator sections of arbitrary matching networks on the signal and idler sides. As we saw in Section IV A, filter sections are either parallel or series resonators yet the tee looks like neither. To begin our synthesis procedure, we transform the physical topology of this core element into an effective circuit comprised of two parallel LC resonators, sandwiching a pure parametric J-type inverter. The steps for this transformation are summarized in Fig. 24(b). Indexing the filter sections from the core outwards, these resonators form the respective k = 1 sections of extended matching networks on the signal (labeled with subscript s) and idler (labeled with i) sides. Following Section IV A, we can relate the impedance of these sections to the reference impedances of the filter networks, Z m,0 :
div.formula: Z m,1 = ω m L m,1 (C2) = ω m ∆ω m Z m,0 g m,1 ,(C3)
div.p: where g m,k is the prototype coefficient corresponding to the kth section in the mth circuit, and with m ∈ {s, i}.
Note that the reference impedances for the respective signal and idler circuits, Z m,0 , as extracted from Eq. C3 are not generally going to be 50 Ω.

With the reference impedance found from Eq. (C3) and the prototype coefficients {g m,k }, we can proceed to derive a circuit with alternating series/parallel resonators (Section IV A). As in the degenerate amplifier example above, the 2-port scattering parameters can be calculated simply by computing the total transmission matrix product, however in this case, the signal and idler modes correspond to separate physical circuits and ports.

Figure 25(a) shows a denormalized ladder circuit (alternating series/parallel resonators) computed for the 3pole (0.5 dB ripple) Chebyshev prototype we have already used in Sec. V D 1. Component values are indicated in the figure. Note that the terminating impedances of the signal port Z 1 and the idler port Z 2 are not 50 Ω; these can be transformed to 50 Ω by inserting admittance inverters as in, e.g., Sec. V D 2. Note also that the impedances of the series resonators in the circuit are rather high, producing inconvenient component values. These sections could be transformed to parallel resonators by use of additional inverters following Sec. IV B. The addition of physical admittance inverters, however, will invariably introduce additional frequency dependence in the circuit [57] that will become more pronounced in designs with either higher bandwidth, or greater frequency separation between the signal and the idler.

Figure 25(b) shows reflection and transmission gain for the circuit in panel (a), computed from its cascaded ABCD matrix. In contrast to Figure 20(b), these represent the power gain rather than the photon number gain, therefore S 21 has an extra overall relative scaling factor f 2 Z 2 /f 1 Z 1 (4.4 dB at 5.0 GHz). Also visible is a sloped gain profile, stemming from a frequency dependence of the parametric inverter itself; this effect is inherent to all physical implementations of nondegenerate parametric amplifiers [42], but is absent in calculations based on ideal (frequency-independent) coupling rates, such as in Fig. 20. Methods for equalizing the gain slope can be found in, e.g., Ref. [71]. Alternatively one can slightly detune the k = 1 resonators [42].

div.head: Modulation amplitude, |δM |
div.p: Here we show how to calculate the modulation amplitude in a general multi-pole parametric amplifier circuit. Knowledge of this parameter is useful for a few reasons. First, it gives the designer a basis for setting requirements on physical parameters such as the participation ratio of the modulated inductance to the total inductance, or the on-chip coupling strength of the ac bias line to the modulated component. Second, it helps determine whether the required inverter constant (in this example J m , as defined in Sec. III C and Figs. 8 and9) is even physically tenable for a given Josephson circuit.

In Figures 8 and9, we parameterize the inverter constants and effective inductances in terms of a normalized modulation parameter α. An expression for α can be derived by requiring that the parametric inverter, when looking from either its signal or idler side, transforms the opposite port's immittance to the correct value to yield the required reflection gain on resonance. For instance, looking into the J inverter from the signal side on resonance, as in Fig. 24(c) right, we see an input admittance, Eq. ( 40), can be written as
div.formula: Y s,in (ω = ω s0 ) = - J s * J i Y i,Ni+1 (C4) = - α ω s0 L s ω i0 L i Y i,Ni+1 .(C5)
div.p: Here, ω s0 and ω i0 are the signal and idler center frequencies, Y i,Ni+1 is the idler termination admittance, and we have used the definitions in Fig. 9
div.formula: Y s,in (ω = ω s0 ) = Y s,Ns+1 √ G -1 √ G + 1 ,(C6)
div.p: we can, after recognizing the terms in the denominator of Eq. (C5) as denormalized filter section immittances, derive a general form for the modulation amplitude,
div.formula: α = |δM | 2 4L s,1 L i,1(C7)
= √ G + 1 √ G -1 ±1 ∆ω s ω s0 ∆ω i ω i,0 g ps s,Ns+1 g pi i,Ni+1 g s,1 g i,1 .(C8)
div.p: The positive and negative signs in the exponent of the gain factor correspond to the use of series/K-type and shunt/J-type inverters, respectively. Likewise, Eq. (C8) is generalized to arbitrary filter orders for the signal/idler circuits, N s/i , different filter prototype coefficients, and differing fractional bandwidth specifications ∆ω m /ω m0 . The p s/i are parity exponents that evaluate to p m = 1 when N m is even, and p m = -1 when N m is odd. This result expands on the examples we have given in the main text, where the same bandwidth and prototype was used for both signal and idler. Since α is physically bounded by the k = 1 mode inductances, α ≤ 1, Eq. (C8) sets limits to the available bandwidth. Likewise, for a fixed bandwidth and gain, one finds that the normalized modulation amplitude √ α gets smaller at increasing prototype order. In practical terms, this means that it gets easier to achieve the target gain as one increases the order of the matching network.

div.head: Appendix D: Inverters at terminations
div.p: The canonical inverter realization consists of either a π network (Fig. 11) or a tee network where the central element (a series or shunt reactance) is sandwiched between (shunt or series) elements with identical reactance values, except with the opposite sign [28,48]. When placed between two resonator circuits, these negative reactances can be absorbed into the reactances forming adjacent resonator sections. However, when an inverter is placed at a real termination, Z 0 (Figure 26), there is no reactance at the termination to absorb the negative reactance. In this tutorial, we follow the standard practice and use an asymmetric 2-element topology that places the negative reactance on one side [57]. One can equate the real and imaginary parts of the input admittance of the ideal inverted termination (Fig. 26, left) to the capacitive 2-element topology (Fig. 26, right), and derive for the series coupling capacitance,
shunted by a negative reactance, -C 01e where

Here we have set ω = ω 0 so that this transformation is exact at the center frequency. This particular topology is similar to the capacitive π inverter, with a leg removed. The expressions are similar to the symmetric π version, Fig. 11(b), but with a scaling factor that is dependent on the desired transformation ratio, since Z 2 0 J 2 01 = Y in /Y 0 (with Z 0 ≡ 1/Y 0 ). One can derive a similar variant for the tee topology using the same approach. As in the canonical π and tee topologies, this inverter is constructed with frequency-dependent reactances, so the inversion is only exactly correct at the center frequency ω 0 . Here, we briefly review the procedure for calculating prototype coefficients using the well-known insertion loss technique (c.f. Ref. 28) and Cauer synthesis [45]. The technique is based on the fact that the input impedance of a ladder circuit, as a function of frequency, can be expressed as a continued-fraction, Eq. (E7). Since prototype tables for passively-terminated filter networks are commonly available [28,57], we will focus here on prototypes designed specifically for active, negative resistance loads, such as presented by parametric amplifiers.

We start with a power loss function, P L (ω), which is an even function of frequency, normalized to a cutoff frequency of 2π×1 Hz. This function is usually expressed as a polynomial of order 2N , where N usually corresponds to the number of reactances (for low-pass networks) or resonant modes (for band-pass networks) in the circuit. The reflected power at the input of the network is given by:
For Butterworth networks, P L can be written as:
div.formula: P L (ω) = A 1 + k 2 ω 2N ,(E2)
div.p: and for Chebyshev networks, it can be written as:
div.formula: P L (ω) = A 1 + k 2 T 2 N (ω) ,(E3)
div.p: where T N (ω) is a Chebyshev polynomial of order N . In both expressions, A ∈ [0, 1] controls the match (P L = 1 means zero reflection), and k 2 controls the reflection at the cutoff frequency where ω = 1.

div.head: Cauer synthesis
div.p: Our task is to factor Eq. (E1) into |Γ(s)| 2 = Γ(s)Γ(-s), where s = jω. Note that we have a choice of sign for Γ: if we choose a positive sign, then when ω → ∞ the low-pass prototype looks like an open circuit, and we may use Γ(s) to write the input impedance of the network (normalized to 1 Ω) Z(s) = 1+Γ(s) 1-Γ(s) . If we choose the negative sign, then when ω → ∞ the low-pass prototype looks like a short circuit, and we may use Γ(s) instead to write the input admittance of the network (normalized to 1 Ω -1 ) Y (s) = 1-Γ(s) 1+Γ(s) . Since |Γ(s)| 2 is a ratio of polynomials of order 2N , Γ(s) is a ratio of polynomials of order N , Γ(s) = R(s)/D(s), where:
div.formula: R(s) = N i=1 (s -z i ) (E4) D(s) = N i=1 (s -p i ) ,(E5)
div.p: where p i are the complex roots of the denominator of |Γ(s)| 2 that are in the left-half-plane (LHP), Re{p i } < 0. z i are the complex roots of the numerator of |Γ(s)| 2 , which we pick either from the LHP or from the imaginary axis in conjugate pairs, or from the origin, until we have exactly N of them.
We can then write (for positive sign of Γ)

or Y (s) = 1/Z(s) (for a negative sign choice). Expanding out Eqs. (E4) and (E5) to polynomial form and plugging into Eq. (E6), we will get a ratio of polynomials where the order of the numerator is greater than that of the denominator by 1.
From here, we proceed to use polynomial long division to write Z(s), or Y (s), as a continued fraction expansion (Cauer form):

The coefficients {g i } of the Cauer expansion are the filter prototype coefficients. The procedure for writing the continued-fraction expansion for a fraction of polynomials and extracting {g i } can be automated using e.g. the numpy python package, where the inputs n and d to the function below are the numerator and denominator polynomials of the impedance (or the admittance), Eq. (E6), respectively: In Section V D we saw that the reflection gain from an active negative-resistance load is equivalent to the return loss from a passive termination having the same absolute value of resistance. However, gain in the signal mode also necessitates gain in the idler mode, and because the amplified signals are correlated [72], the power loss function that we need to use when synthesizing a matching network should be calculated based on the gain parameter:
div.formula: import
div.p: where G is the signal power gain. This is in contrast to Ref. 47, which assumes that the amplified signal and idler are not correlated. In other words, if P s is the input signal power, then G P L P s is the power extracted from the pump. The matching network must be designed to allow this power to be dissipated in both the signal and idler terminations.

div.head: a. Butterworth response
div.p: For the Butterworth response type, we set A in Eq. (E2) so that |Γ(0)| 2 = 1/G P L , and k 2 in Eq. (E2) so that at the cutoff frequency |Γ(1)| 2 = 2/G P L , to define the amplifier bandwidth as the half-gain points (3 dB bandwidth).
The power loss function we use to generate normalized Butterworth low-pass prototypes of order N for a desired signal power gain G is therefore given by:
div.formula: P L (ω) = G P L G P L -1 1 + 1 G P L -2 ω 2N ,(E9)
div.p: where G P L is defined in Eq. (E8). Calculated prototype coefficients for a practical selection of gains are listed in Table . II.

div.head: b. Chebyshev response
div.p: For the Chebyshev response type, for a given order N , and specified signal gain G 0 in dB, and gain ripple R in dB, we define:
div.formula: G dB min = G 0 ; N ∈ even G 0 -R; N ∈ odd ,(E10)
div.p: and:
div.formula: G dB max = G 0 + R; N ∈ even G 0 ; N ∈ odd . (E11
div.p: If G max and G min are the corresponding signal power gains in linear power units, then we should calculate the power loss function in terms of G P L max = 4G max -2 and G P L min = 4G min -2, similar to Eq. (E8). The power loss function can then be written as:
div.formula: P L (ω) = G P L max G P L max -1 1 + 1 G P L min - 1 G P L max T 2 N (ω) .
div.p: (E12) Note that for Chebyshev response, the parameter k 2 in Eq. (E3) does not correspond to the 3 dB (half gain) points, but rather, it controls the bandwidth over which the gain oscillates by the ripple amount [28]. Therefore the 3 dB bandwidth of the amplifier will generally be greater than the specified bandwidth parameter ∆ω that appears in Sections IV and V. Calculated prototype coefficients for a practical selection of gain and ripple specifications are listed in Table . III. Additional listings are given in Refs. 47 and 57, however the gain specified therein is the total gain in both signal and idler bands, leading roughly to a 6 dB difference from our definition: what we denote as G = 20 dB, would appear as G ≈ 26 dB in the convention of Refs. 47 and 57.
Appendix F: Cauer form of a 1D array of nearest-neighbor coupled modes using graph reduction
We start by looking at the example of Fig. 7 for a four-mode circuit and then generalize. Eq. ( 27) writes the reduced-mode detuning term for the system as:
For a 1D simply-connected system, we can take β jk to be real, so we drop the absolute value in |β jk | 2 compared to Eq. ( 27).
We will make the resonant pump assumption, and change variable to a normalized detuning, as common in microwave engineering:
where ω is the signal frequency, ω 0 is the mode frequency, and ∆ω is the bandwidth. With this substitution we can write the detuning terms appearing in Eq. (F1) as follows:
Plugging this into Eq. (F1) and using Eq. ( 26), we can write the admittance seen from the port (after some algebra) as:

where we have defined: We further define g 0 as the prefactor in Eq. (F6). This brings the continued-fraction expression for the admit-tance, Eq. (F6), to exact equivalence with the Cauer expansion in Appendix. E. The coefficients {g i } are the prototype coefficients of the normalized low-pass network (c.f. Sec. IV) that represents the circuit. Looking at products of the form g j g j+1 , and generalizing to a 1D coupled system of N modes:
div.formula: g 0 g 1 = ∆ω γ 01 , g j g j+1 = 1 β 2 j,j+1

Appendix A: Z, Y , and ABCD matrix representations for modulated inductors

In Section III we wrote the Z matrices for parametrically pumped circuit elements. Here we will show how to transform those to ABCD (T) matrices and factor the component into passive and purely-parametric parts, as we have done in Figs. 8 and9.

div.head: Equivalent shunt inverter circuits
div.p: In building a frequency converter or amplifier circuit, L 0 should be regarded as part of the core resonator and corresponding matching sections, which we term the signal and idler matching networks, each evaluated at their respective frequencies. In the series representation, L 0 is used to build the last series LC section in a multipole matching network, up to the inverter. However, it is often the case that parallel LC sections are easier to implement in a given technology [28,48]. To convert to a parallel representation, one can write down a transmission matrix factorization, similar to (A1), but for the admittance matrix
Applying Eq. (A5) with the inverted Z-matrices from Sec. III yields an equivalent shunt representation (see Figure 8(b)),
and
where we have defined an effective shunt inductance
and inverter constant
The key results of this Appendix are summarized, in compact form, in Fig. 8(b). Similar arguments can be used to derive the relations summarized in Fig. 9(b) for the modulated mutual inductance.

div.head: Appendix B: Josephson Junctions and SQUIDs as modulated inductors
div.p: The role of the parametric coupler element in Section III is played by a modulated inductor, which in Josephson parametric devices, can be performed by any of a variety of single and multi-junction devices [7,8,39,51]. Here we give a primer on two very simple choices, a single Josephson junction and the dc-SQUID.
